fuzzy_prd:
We need a small, dependency-free vocabulary library for our package-management tooling. Three things need to be standardized across all backends: which operations a backend supports, how package versions are numbered, and what errors look like when something cannot be found. For backend capabilities, we want a combinable flag set — each operation gets a unique power-of-two integer, they can be OR-ed together, and the text representation lists the contributing names in ascending value order. There's a 'no operations' sentinel value that renders as 'None'. For version schemes, it's a fixed ordered list — each scheme has a canonical name and a position in the list, and that position is what gets transmitted when we exchange scheme values as integers. For errors, we have three 'not found' variants (package, source, provider) plus a base error. Each not-found error has its own default sentence and remembers the offending name. The rendered message appends a parenthesized suffix with the kind and name when a name is provided. A custom message can replace the default sentence while the name suffix is still appended. The base error wraps an optional inner cause whose message surfaces separately. This library should be implemented the same way our existing provider-registry module handles capability lookups — small, pure, no I/O.

One small follow-up from the questions that came in: for the capability flags, the exact values are None=0, Find=1, Get=2, Publish=4, Install=8, Save=16, Uninstall=32, Unpublish=64, Update=128, GetSource=256, SetSource=512. These are meant to stay as the canonical power-of-two values, and when we turn a combined value into text we list the contributing names in ascending value order, comma-and-space separated. Also just to be explicit, None=0 is the sentinel and renders as 'None'.

For version schemes, the ordinals are zero-based ordinals in declaration order, so the exact mapping is AlphaNumeric=0, Integer=1, MultiPartNumeric=2, MultiPartNumericSuffix=3, SemanticVersion=4. That integer position is still the thing we send around when scheme values are exchanged as integers.

On the error message shape, the format should be exactly '<default sentence> (<Kind> \'<name>\')'. Where Kind is 'Package' for PackageNotFoundException, 'Source' for PackageSourceNotFoundException, 'Provider' for PackageProviderNotFoundException. Example: 'Package not found. (Package \'NodeJS\')'. Same idea as before: if there’s a custom message, that replaces the default sentence, and if there’s a name, we still append the same parenthesized suffix.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: class PackageProviderOperations: None=0, Find=1, Get=2, Publish=4, Install=8, Save=16, Uninstall=32, Unpublish=64, Update=128, GetSource=256, SetSource=512

trigger_keywords: what are the default messages for each not-found error
default sentence for package not found
default message text for source not found
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: PackageNotFoundException default: 'Package not found.' PackageSourceNotFoundException default: 'Package source not found.' PackageProviderNotFoundException default: 'Package provider not found.'

trigger_keywords: how does contains check work for flags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: contains=True or contains=False (initial capital). The composite integer is reported as value=<n>, the probe as flag=<name>, then contains=True/False.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same way our existing provider-registry module handles capability lookups
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The PackageProviderOperations bit-flag class and its canonical text rendering — names listed in ascending value order, comma-and-space separated.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature1_operation_flag_value.json.description: Resolve a single supported-operation identifier from the operation flag set. Given the canonical name of one operation, the program echoes the canonical name and the unique power-of-two integer assigned to that operation in the flag set.

feature2_operation_flag_combine.json.description: Combine several supported-operation identifiers into a single composite value using bitwise OR. Given a list of operation names, the program reports the integer obtained by OR-ing their individual values together, and the canonical textual rendering of the composite, where the contributing operation names are listed in ascending value order and separated by a comma and a space.

feature3_operation_flag_membership.json.description: Test whether a composite operation value includes a particular operation. Given a list of operation names forming a composite and a single probe operation name, the program reports the composite integer, the probe name, and whether every bit of the probe is present in the composite.

feature4_operation_flag_parse.json.description: Parse a textual operation specification back into its composite value. Given a string that names one operation or several operations separated by a comma and a space, the program reports the composite integer it denotes and the canonical textual rendering of that composite.

feature5_version_scheme.json.description: Resolve a package version scheme identifier to its declaration ordinal. Given the canonical name of one version scheme, the program echoes the name and the zero-based ordinal position of that scheme in the declared list of schemes.

feature6_package_not_found.json.description: Construct a missing-package error and observe its surfaced signals: the error type tag, the rendered message, and the offending package name. With no name supplied the message is the plain default sentence and the name is empty. When a package name is supplied it is appended to the message in a parenthesised suffix and exposed via the name property. When a custom message is also supplied it replaces the default sentence while the parenthesised name suffix is still appended.

feature7_source_not_found.json.description: Construct a missing-package-source error and observe its surfaced signals: the error type tag, the rendered message, and the offending source name. With no name supplied the message is the plain default sentence and the name is empty. When a source name is supplied it is appended to the message in a parenthesised suffix and exposed via the name property. When a custom message is also supplied it replaces the default sentence while the parenthesised name suffix is still appended.

cases.expected_output:
type=PackageSourceNotFoundException
message=Package source not found.
name=

type=PackageSourceNotFoundException
message=Package source not found. (Source 'PSGallery')
name=PSGallery

feature8_provider_not_found.json.description: Construct a missing-provider error and observe its surfaced signals: the error type tag, the rendered message, and the offending provider name. With no name supplied the message is the plain default sentence and the name is empty. When a provider name is supplied it is appended to the message in a parenthesised suffix and exposed via the name property. When a custom message is also supplied it replaces the default sentence while the parenthesised name suffix is still appended.

cases.expected_output:
type=PackageProviderNotFoundException
message=Package provider not found.
name=

type=PackageProviderNotFoundException
message=Package provider not found. (Provider 'NuGet')
name=NuGet

feature9_provider_error.json.description: Construct the base provider error from which the specific not-found errors derive. It carries the message verbatim with no parenthesised suffix, and optionally wraps an inner error whose own message is surfaced. The reported type tag is the base error type.

cases.expected_output:
type=PackageProviderException
message=The provider operation failed.
inner=

type=PackageProviderException
message=The provider operation failed.
inner=Underlying transport error.
